name: Hebe
description: Hebe, in Greek mythology, is the goddess of youth, daughter of Zeus and Hera. She served as the cupbearer for the gods and goddesses of Mount Olympus, serving ambrosia and nectar, ensuring their eternal youth and vitality. She is often depicted as a beautiful young woman, sometimes winged, pouring drinks from a pitcher. Her role extended beyond mere service; she was the embodiment of youthful bloom and the divine right of immortality. Before Ganymede took over, Hebe held the esteemed position of cupbearer, highlighting her importance within the Olympian hierarchy. She is associated with weddings and new beginnings, symbolizing the freshness and vigor of youth. Eventually, Hebe married Heracles after his apotheosis, signifying a divine union of youth and strength, and bringing an end to her service as cupbearer. She is revered for her grace, beauty, and the life-sustaining ambrosia she provided to the gods, ensuring their everlasting existence. Hebe represents the eternal cycle of life, the invigorating power of youth, and the promise of immortality through divine favor.
subtitle: Goddess of Youth and Cupbearer

keyMyths.title: Hebe as Cupbearer
keyMyths.description: Hebe served as the cupbearer to the Olympian gods, providing them with ambrosia and nectar, the source of their immortality. This role highlighted her importance and connection to the gods' eternal life.

keyMyths.title: Marriage to Heracles
keyMyths.description: After Heracles completed his labors and ascended to Olympus, he married Hebe, signifying a union of strength and youth. This marked the end of Hebe's service as cupbearer.

keyMyths.title: Resignation as Cupbearer
keyMyths.description: Hebe resigned or was replaced as cupbearer, some accounts say due to clumsiness, and Ganymede, a Trojan prince, took her place. This change is a significant point in her mythology.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: References to Hebe's beauty and role on Olympus.
sources.text: References to Hebe's beauty and role on Olympus.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: Account of her marriage to Heracles.
sources.text: Account of her marriage to Heracles.

worshipPractices.name: Libations
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of liquids, such as wine or milk, were poured out to honor Hebe, reflecting her role as a cupbearer.

worshipPractices.name: Statues and Altars
worshipPractices.description: Statues and altars were dedicated to Hebe in temples and sacred spaces, where worshippers would offer prayers and sacrifices.

festivals.name: Hebeia
festivals.description: A festival dedicated to Hebe, celebrating youth and vitality. Details are scarce, but it likely involved athletic competitions and feasts.

cultural.modernLegacy: Hebe's influence is subtle but present in modern concepts of youth, beauty, and vitality. Her image is sometimes invoked in advertising and art, representing the ideals of youthful exuberance and beauty. Her name is occasionally used, particularly in botanical contexts, reflecting her association with growth and freshness.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Hebe's parentage is straightforward in most accounts: she is the daughter of Zeus, king of the gods, and Hera, queen of the gods and goddess of marriage. This places her in a position of considerable importance within the Olympian pantheon from birth. Her birth is not typically surrounded by specific myths or dramatic events, unlike some other deities. Instead, her significance arises from her role and her inherent qualities. As the offspring of the two most powerful deities, Hebe embodies the ideal of divine youth and beauty, destined to serve a crucial function in maintaining the gods' immortality and vitality. Her very existence is a testament to the enduring power and regenerative capacity of the Olympian order.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Hebe's primary role is as the cupbearer to the gods, serving ambrosia and nectar at their feasts on Mount Olympus. This duty ensured the gods' eternal youth and immortality. While not a central figure in many major myths, her actions have important consequences. One significant story involves her eventual marriage to Heracles (Hercules) after he ascends to Olympus. This union symbolizes the hero's attainment of immortality and divine status, marking the end of his mortal trials. Hebe's departure from her cupbearing duties is another notable event, with Ganymede, a Trojan prince, taking her place. This change is sometimes attributed to Hebe's marriage or a desire to focus on other aspects of her divinity. Although not a protagonist in grand narratives, Hebe's presence is essential to the smooth functioning of the divine realm.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Worship of Hebe was not as widespread or prominent as that of other major Olympian deities. However, she did receive veneration in specific regions and contexts. Shrines and altars dedicated to her were established, where worshippers offered libations and prayers. Her association with youth and vitality likely made her a popular figure for those seeking blessings of health and vigor. While dedicated festivals specifically honoring Hebe are not extensively documented, it is plausible that she was included in broader celebrations of the Olympian gods. The limited evidence suggests a more localized and personal form of worship, focusing on her role as a giver of youth and a symbol of eternal life. Her temples were often adorned with symbols of youth and renewal, further emphasizing her divine domain.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Hebe's legacy is primarily symbolic, representing the enduring ideals of youth, beauty, and vitality. While not a major figure in modern popular culture, her image and attributes continue to resonate in various forms. She appears in classical art and literature, often depicted as a beautiful young woman pouring drinks, embodying grace and youthful exuberance. Her name is sometimes used in botanical nomenclature, reflecting her association with growth and freshness. In a broader sense, Hebe's influence can be seen in the ongoing cultural fascination with youthfulness and the pursuit of eternal life. She serves as a reminder of the timeless human desire to maintain vigor and beauty, even in the face of mortality. Her role as a cupbearer, ensuring the gods' immortality, continues to hold symbolic power in discussions of health, wellness, and the pursuit of longevity.

shortDescription: Greek goddess of youth, cupbearer to the gods, and daughter of Zeus and Hera. She embodies eternal youth and vitality.
